text: The video displays a large piece of retroperitoneal fat that is revealed within the spermatic cord.
The surgeon operates in the space of Bogros, navigating through the natural tissue plane with visible precision.
The surgeon dissects laterally, directly onto the peritoneum, carefully following the natural tissue plane.
The surgeon inserts an additional port into the surgical field, visible on the screen.
The surgeon uses a Cartier instrument for retraction in the scrotal area, adding a third instrument to assist in the procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the spermatic cord, revealing a significant amount of surrounding adipose tissue.
The surgeon observes an abnormally abundant amount of fat surrounding the spermatic cord, considering the need to reduce this fat, which is atypical for inguinal hernia procedures.
The surgical field shows the spermatic cord surrounded by abundant fat, with no large hernia sac visible.
The surgeon identifies a column of fat surrounding the spermatic cord that requires reduction, highlighting its prominence within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully manipulates tissue around the cord structures near the internal ring, focusing on the surrounding anatomical area.
The surgeon attempts to reduce the abnormally abundant fat identified in the surgical field.
The surgeon's bedside assistant compresses the scrotum to aid in the reduction of hernia contents during the procedure.
The surgeon has opened a fascial layer, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon attempts to reduce the anterior yellowish fat, revealing a distinct plane between the fat and the underlying structure, which contains multiple long vessels coursing along it.
The surgeon identifies a clear point for division of the yellowish fat, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon proceeds to divide the yellowish fat, revealing a clear plane between the fat and the underlying structure with multiple long vessels.
The surgeon's bedside assistant attempts to reduce the anterior yellowish fat, revealing a clear plane with multiple long vessels before the surgeon proceeds with complete division.
The surgeon gently pulls on the structure with multiple long vessels coursing along it, visible behind the anterior yellowish fat.
The surgeon gently pulls on the tissue, obtaining a slightly larger portion of the mass for further examination.
The surgeon identifies a suitable point on the tissue for division, preparing to separate the fat mass.
The hernia sac appears to be completely reduced, with no visible protrusion remaining in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the hernia sac, carefully prioritizing the preservation of the spermatic cord.
The surgeon checks the tip of the hernia sac, identifying the last portion of the sac located medially.
The surgeon anticipates the vas deferens will soon become visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to parietalize the spermatic cord, focusing on separating it from surrounding tissues during the hernia dissection.
The surgeon visually identifies the vas deferens within the surgical field.
A distinct yellow piece of fat is visible in the surgical field, contrasting with surrounding tissues.
The surgeon begins dissection in the area near the testicular vessels, carefully avoiding potential nerve-like structures.
The surgeon observes a piece of yellow fat that appears intimately involved with the testicular vessels, indicating a close anatomical relationship between the fat and the vessels.
The surgeon attempts to separate the structure from the testicular vessels, observing the interaction between the two anatomical elements.
The surgeon carefully dissects to separate the testicular vessels from surrounding fat, ensuring not to penetrate deeply into the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon observes the loose and amorphous appearance of the fat tissue during the dissection, noting its lack of defined structure.
The surgeon observes the fat tissue breaking apart easily during the dissection, indicating its lack of structural integrity.
The surgeon observes the absence of a natural plane of separation between the fat and the testicular vessels, indicating a need for careful dissection to differentiate these structures.
The surgeon reduces a larger amount of amorphous fat, which lacks distinct form, from the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the fat becoming narrower, visually resembling a separate piece distinct from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon leaves a significant amount of fat in the surgical field, visible as a distinct layer within the dissection area.
The surgeon inspects the medial area, observing a column of fat, but upon dissection, determines that the fat cannot be separated from the testicular vessels.
The surgeon visually inspects the surgical field to identify a distal transection point, ensuring separation from the testicular vessels.
The surgeon isolates the testicular vessels by separating surrounding tissue, preparing to paralyze the spermatic cord.
The surgeon divides small vessels, carefully isolating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon visually inspects the surgical field while gently pushing and retracting tissues in various directions to improve exposure.
The surgeon observes the surgical field, noting the minimal remaining tissue available for division.
The surgeon separates a piece of fat from the testicular vessels, dividing small vessels to facilitate the separation.
The surgeon uses a bipolar cautery device to control small vessels, which retract quickly upon coagulation.
The surgeon prepares the surgical site by creating a suitable landing zone for mesh placement, ensuring adequate space and tissue configuration for optimal mesh integration.
The surgeon observes a significant amount of fat tissue in the surgical field, considering the potential risk of herniation if the mesh is placed directly on top of this tissue.
The surgeon continues to separate tissue from the testicular vessels, carefully dissecting to ensure clear separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon observes a piece of fat that appears to be separable from the testicular vessels.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the tissue layers, attempting to visualize the inguinal canal.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field to ensure no additional fat remains, though visibility is challenging.
The surgeon identifies a natural tissue plane adjacent to the testicular vessels, suggesting a potential area for further dissection.
The surgeon creates a plane to thin out the testicular vessels, ensuring that the bulk of the fat cannot slide behind the mesh.
The surgeon identifies a structure that appears to be a lymphatic channel during the dissection.
A large vein becomes visible in the surgical field, emerging prominently among the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon moves anteriorly, preserving a very large vein visible to the left of the scissors.
A tortuous vein is visible, partially obscured by surrounding fat tissue.
The surgeon adjusts the tissue, revealing the large vein once more in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the scissors near a large vein visible to the left, avoiding transection of the vessel.
The surgeon has completely separated the fat, extending the dissection cephalad, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon demonstrates the preservation of a large vein, ensuring it remains intact during the procedure.
The surgeon positions the Progrip mesh over the prepared landing zone, which has been cleared of surrounding tissue.
The surgeon displays a 16x12 cm Progrip mesh, preparing it for placement in the surgical field.

The surgeon tucks the mesh deep into the space of Retzius, ensuring adequate coverage of the anterior portion to cover the top of the defect, while being mindful of the posterior lateral positioning relative to the core structures in the triangle of pain.
The surgeon leaves the surgical materials in the preperitoneal space, maintaining their position without further adjustment.
The surgeon visually identifies a small to medium-sized hernia sac within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the hernia sac to patch the defect after the flap ripped during dissection.
